acceptance_targets: [ch12-craft-001] Between snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_12.md:115 and snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_12.md:149, the revised passage contains no more than one abstract sentence naming Lola's fraud/performance dilemma; every other paragraph in that stretch must either change Tammy's read of Lola, force Lola into a new choice, or sharpen the final memory test that ends with Woodbridge disappearing.

[F01_ch12_continuity_greg] Read lines 2420-2520. Every reference to Tammy's ex-husband uses the same name. The town where the girlfriend works matches the town used in chapter 14. No reader encountering both chapters would notice a discrepancy.

findings.acceptance_test: Between snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_12.md:115 and snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_12.md:149, the revised passage contains no more than one abstract sentence naming Lola's fraud/performance dilemma; every other paragraph in that stretch must either change Tammy's read of Lola, force Lola into a new choice, or sharpen the final memory test that ends with Woodbridge disappearing.
findings.problem: Once Tammy's confession and Lola's "You're a good person, mija" have already landed the emotional turn, the narration keeps translating the same guilt beat into abstract thesis language. The scene stops escalating and starts explaining that Lola is still herself and that confession would ruin everything, which blunts the pressure the dialogue has already made legible.
findings.rewrite_direction: Compress or repurpose the explanatory material at snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_12.md:117, snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_12.md:119, snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_12.md:137, and snapshots/cycle_02/chapters/chapter_12.md:139. Keep Tammy's loneliness, Lola's withheld honesty, and the final Woodbridge loss, but make the space earn itself through visible risk: Tammy's reaction, Lola's bodily effort not to confess, or another immediate consequence instead of abstract paraphrase of the theme.

findings.acceptance_test: Read lines 2420-2520. Every reference to Tammy's ex-husband uses the same name. The town where the girlfriend works matches the town used in chapter 14. No reader encountering both chapters would notice a discrepancy.
findings.evidence: snapshots/cycle_02/FINAL_NOVEL.md:2426, snapshots/cycle_02/FINAL_NOVEL.md:2430, snapshots/cycle_02/FINAL_NOVEL.md:2446, snapshots/cycle_02/FINAL_NOVEL.md:2496, snapshots/cycle_02/FINAL_NOVEL.md:2508, snapshots/cycle_02/FINAL_NOVEL.md:2512

evidence.excerpt: 2426: Tammy drank her coffee and made the face she always made, the one that said this is undrinkable and she was going to finish every drop. "Greg's got a girlfriend."

2430: "Yeah. Brandy. Her name's Brandy, like the — yeah." Tammy's voice was flat in the way that meant she was working hard to keep it flat. "She's twenty-eight. Works at a Verizon store in Woodbridge."
2446: "Greg never forgot an anniversary," Tammy said. "He just forgot to give a shit about the other three hundred and sixty-four days."

2496: Lola looked at her. She wanted to say: I'm not confused. I haven't been confused. I remember every word you've said to me about Greg and Brandy and the apartment and the cat. I'm lying to you every day, every time you brush my hair and bring me coffee and sit on this bench and tell me things that cost you something. I'm lying and you deserve better than a friend who isn't real.

2508: Lola watched the wet rings drying on the bench and tried to keep Tammy's life in order. Greg. Brandy. Verizon. Twenty-eight. Studio. Window facing a wall. She said the list under her breath, the way she'd once made children repeat spelling words until they stuck. Greg. Brandy. Verizon. Twenty-eight. Studio. Window facing a wall.
2512: Not the feeling of it. Just the word. The little Jersey town where Brandy sold phone plans and posed beside Greg in candlelight on Facebook, and Lola could not catch its name.

rewrite_direction.excerpt: 2426: Tammy drank her coffee and made the face she always made, the one that said this is undrinkable and she was going to finish every drop. "Greg's got a girlfriend."

findings.problem: Tammy's ex-husband is named 'Greg' throughout this chapter (lines 2426, 2446, 2496, 2508, 2512) and her ex's new girlfriend Brandy works at 'a Verizon store in Woodbridge' (line 2430). Chapter 14, which was drafted in parallel, calls the ex-husband 'Kevin' and places the girlfriend in 'Bridgewater.' This is a cross-chapter factual inconsistency — the reader encounters two different names for the same character within two chapters.
findings.rewrite_direction: At snapshots/cycle_02/FINAL_NOVEL.md:2426, 2446, 2496, 2508, 2512: the ex-husband's name must be consistent with chapter 14. If this chapter is revised first, use whichever name the revision agent prefers, but flag it for cross-chapter reconciliation. The simplest fix: replace every instance of 'Greg' with 'Kevin' (five occurrences). At line 2430, replace 'Woodbridge' with 'Bridgewater' to match chapter 14's version, OR keep 'Woodbridge' and flag chapter 14 for correction. The name and town must match across both chapters.

non_negotiables: Maintain heading contract exactly (# Chapter N).
Elliptical clipping is not permitted in dialogue or narration.
Avoid transcript cadence in high-stakes dialogue.
Avoid stiff over-formality in dialogue; use contractions and colloquial rhythm where character-true.
Preserve productive dialogue roughness; do not clean living speech into balanced thesis statements.
Reduce procedural drift when it displaces embodied consequence.
Maintain character-specific voice signatures from style bible.
Honor aesthetic risk policy: sanitization is the primary craft risk — do not soften, euphemize, or retreat from the story's established register. When in doubt, err toward rawness.

revision_pass.focus: Prioritize structural and craft blockers first: causality, chapter architecture, stakes clarity, continuity logic, and all HIGH/CRITICAL defects.
revision_pass.label: HIGH/CRITICAL structural/craft fixes

aesthetic_risk_policy.creative_risk_policy: This is a dark comedy about elderly agency, institutional failure, and moral compromise in a setting most fiction treats as a punchline or a tragedy but never both. The risks the novel must take: render the comedy of octogenarians as pharmaceutical distributors without condescending to them or turning them into lovable rogues; make Carmen's escalating complicity fully legible without moralizing about it; show the bodily realities of aging — incontinence, tremor, cognitive decline, falls, the smell of a body that doesn't leave a building — with unsentimental specificity; render the economic desperation of both healthcare workers and uninsured buyers without turning either into social-issue abstractions; give the moral ambiguity of the ring genuine weight by refusing to resolve it into either 'they were wrong' or 'they were heroes.' The novel must trust readers to sit with complexity. When in doubt between too raw and too safe, choose raw. A nursing home that smells like potpourri is a nursing home written by someone who's never been in one.
aesthetic_risk_policy.euphemism_penalty: Euphemism for bodily functions, aging, economic shame, institutional dysfunction, or the realities of nursing home care is a craft failure. The novel's comedy and humanity depend on rendering what polite fiction glosses over: what it smells like to clean someone who can't clean themselves, what it costs to count coins for a bus fare, what oxycodone sells for per milligram on the secondary market, what a concussion looks like on an 88-year-old skull, what it sounds like when someone with Parkinson's tries to hold a coffee cup steady, what Burt's dementia actually looks like up close. If the prose flinches from these specifics, the novel loses its authority.

dialogue_rules.default_contraction_use: High — contractions are the default for all dialogue. Uncontracted forms appear only for emphasis, character-specific formality (Agnes's low contraction level), Carmen under extreme stress reverting to formal speech, or deliberate register choices. In a contemporary New Jersey nursing home, stiff diction is the anomaly, not the norm.

prose_style_profile.chapter_texture_variance: Alternate high-intensity scenes with reflective interiority. Vary scene count: single-scene chapters for maximum compression, three-scene chapters for range. Comic chapters (3, 9) should feel structurally different from quiet character studies (7, 8, 12, 15), which should feel different from crisis chapters (11, 16, 17). Follow a confrontation with a reflection, an escalation with a domestic scene, a comic set piece with an elegiac one. If two consecutive chapters share the same rhythm, one needs redesigning.
prose_style_profile.diction: Grounded and specific. Drug names (oxycodone, alprazolam, hydroxychloroquine), nursing procedures (med pass, pill count, charting, fall assessment), body realities (incontinence, tremor, laceration, scalp bleed). No purple prose, no literary performance. The style should feel like someone who works with their hands is telling you the truth about what she saw.
prose_style_profile.narrative_distance: Close third person, varying by POV character. Carmen chapters are warm and sensory, anchored in the body and the immediate environment. Harry chapters are precise and calculating, with attention to numbers and angles. Agnes chapters are clinical and compressed. Lola chapters are winding and emotionally layered, with embedded memories. Earl chapters are spare and weighted, with silence doing as much work as language. Marcus chapters are kinetic, with physical movement and code-switching. Tammy chapters are flat and observational, her trained eye cataloguing what doesn't add up.
prose_style_profile.rhythm_target: Varied by chapter and POV. Carmen chapters favor medium sentences with occasional long runs when she's anxious. Harry chapters are short and clipped. Comic set pieces (chapters 3 and 9) move between fast inventory and slow domestic beats. Quiet chapters (7, 12, 15) slow down and let space onto the page. No chapter should feel rhythmically identical to the one before it.
